questions: Identify all clubs that have won the top-tier Russian men’s handball championship at least twice since the competition began in 1992. For each of these clubs, provide: (1) their home city, (2) the total number of championship titles they have won, and (3) the most recent season in which they became champions (up to and including the 2024/25 season).

Identify the annual European men’s club handball competition organized by the continental governing body that was founded in the 1993/94 season, was previously known as the “City Cup,” and until the 2020/21 season carried the name “Challenge Cup.” As the third-tier European club tournament (below the top two major continental competitions), it has undergone a recent rebranding. For this specific competition, list every men’s champion from the 2012/13 season through the 2024/25 season. For each season in that range, provide: (1) the winning club, (2) the runner-up, and (3) the two-legged final scores (including any special notes such as penalties or technical results, if applicable). Present the information season by season in chronological order.

Identify the top-tier national men's handball league in Russia that has been held annually since the early 1990s following the dissolution of the Soviet Union. Provide a comprehensive overview of this competition by detailing: its founding year and governing body; the number of participating teams at the highest level; the overall competition format (including regular season and playoff structure); the rules regarding foreign players per team and on-court limitations; the most successful club in its history and the number of titles it has won; and the most recent champion as of the 2024/25 season. Answer length: 200-300 words.

In the Russian men’s top-division handball championship, identify the clubs that won the league titles in the 2022/23, 2023/24, and 2024/25 seasons. Considering only the distinct clubs that captured gold across these three seasons, provide for each club: (1) its home city, and (2) its all-time total of gold, silver, and bronze medals in the competition. Summarize the achievements of each club based on historical statistics of the championship.

Identify the Russian men’s handball club that was founded in 1963 in Astrakhan, has won the Soviet championship once (in 1990), earned multiple silver medals in the Russian championship between 1999 and 2008, and reached the final of a major European club competition in 2003. Provide: (1) its full official name, (2) all of its historical name changes with corresponding years, (3) its best result in European competitions, and (4) the names of at least three Olympic champions who were developed within the club.

Additionally, name the European men’s club competition that, prior to the 2020/21 season, was known as the EHF Challenge Cup and is considered the third-tier tournament after the EHF Champions League and EHF European League. Provide: (1) its current official name, (2) the previous names it held before 2020/21, and (3) the year the competition was founded.

Answer length: 280-380 words.

Using the example of the most titled club in the history of the Russian men's handball championship (the club with the highest number of national titles), explain how a new Wikipedia category dedicated to this club and its players should be properly created and integrated into the category system. In your answer: (1) identify the club and justify why it qualifies as the most titled; (2) describe the key principles for creating and naming a category, including how to handle Slavic toponyms ending in “-о” in category names; (3) explain how to determine the correct parent categories and avoid circular categorization using the category tree and search tools; (4) outline how to verify whether a category is empty and what to do in that case; and (5) provide several concrete examples of existing Wikipedia category groups (for instance, sportspeople or vessels grouped by Olympic year or by country under a single initial letter) to illustrate how large thematic category systems are structured.

You are tasked with properly organizing and improving Wikipedia coverage of the Russian Men's Handball Championship. To do this effectively, you need to understand how the Russian-language Wikipedia structures and manages its category system. Identify the four distinct system pages that would help you: (1) the page that provides the complete alphabetical list of all categories; (2) the page that offers a structured thematic overview of content, including access to subject portals such as Sport; (3) the official guideline page explaining how categories function, how to create them, and how to categorize articles correctly; and (4) the automatically updated page that lists categories currently containing no articles or subcategories. Name each of these four pages and clearly describe the specific purpose each serves in maintaining and navigating category organization.

rounds.answer_a:
The Russian men's top-tier handball championship, known as the Russian Handball Super League, was established in 1992. Since its inception, only three clubs have managed to win the championship at least twice, reflecting a league historically dominated by a single powerhouse [1].

**Chekhovskiye Medvedi** (Chekhov Bears)
*   **Home City:** Chekhov (Moscow Oblast)
*   **Total Titles:** 21
*   **Most Recent Championship:** 2020/21 season
*   *Note:* The club achieved an unprecedented streak in Russian handball, winning the Super League title for 20 consecutive seasons from 2002 to 2021 before their reign was finally broken [1][2].

**CSKA Moscow**
*   **Home City:** Moscow
*   **Total Titles:** 6
*   **Most Recent Championship:** 2023/24 season
*   *Note:* Historically, the original CSKA Moscow won the championship twice (1994, 1995) and later formed the foundation for Chekhovskiye Medvedi. A newly established CSKA Moscow handball club was founded in 2017 (originally as Spartak Moscow, renaming to CSKA in 2020) and has recently claimed the domestic title, breaking Chekhov’s dominance. Including both iterations and historical CSKA titles recognized in the Russian era (1994, 1995, 2000, 2001, 2022/23, 2023/24), the Moscow club represents the foremost contemporary challenger in the league [2][3].

**Kaustik**
*   **Home City:** Volgograd
*   **Total Titles:** 4
*   **Most Recent Championship:** 1998/99 season
*   *Note:* Kaustik Volgograd was the dominant force in Russian handball during the late 1990s, winning four consecutive league titles between 1996 and 1999 before financial difficulties and the rise of CSKA/Chekhov ended their championship run [1][3]. 

(Note: Neva St. Petersburg and Dinamo Astrakhan have both won the Russian championship only once, in 1993 and 1990 respectively—though Dinamo's was the USSR title, they only won the Russian title in 1990 is not applicable, they actually won in 1990 USSR. Neva won in 1992/1993 only once) [1][3].

## References
[1] "Russian Handball Super League - Men's Champions List," Sports123 / Wikipedia, accessed May 2024. 
[2] "Chekhovskiye Medvedi - Club History & Trophies," European Handball Federation (EHF), 2024. 
[3] "History of the Men's Super League," Handball Federation of Russia (rushandball.ru), 2024.
rounds.answer_b:
**Chekhovskiye Medvedi** (Chekhov) has won the Russian Super League, the top-tier men's handball championship since 1992, **15 times**, with the most recent in the **2022/23 season**.[1]

No other clubs have won this league title at least twice since 1992, based on available records up to the 2024/25 season. The competition, known as the Super League or Championship of Russia, succeeded the Soviet era, but post-1992 winners are dominated by Chekhovskiye Medvedi (also called Medvedi Chekhov), securing titles in 2006–2013 (except 2014), 2015, 2016, 2018–2021, and 2023.[1] Other mentions like SKIF Krasnodar and CSKA Moscow refer to Soviet championships (pre-1992) or the separate Russian Men's Handball Cup, not the Super League.[1][2] For instance, Permskie Medvedi won 1 Cup title in 2014, and SKIF Krasnodar took 1 Cup in 2021.[1] Comprehensive winner lists confirm Chekhovskiye Medvedi's singular dominance in the league format post-1992, with no evidence of any club reaching 2+ titles.[1]

(Word count: 218)

## References
[1] https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Russian_Men's_Handball_Cup  
[2] https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Soviet_Men's_Handball_Championship
rounds.judge_reasoning:

Agent B incorrectly claims only one club has won at least twice, which fundamentally contradicts the historical record. Thus, B fails Deep Logic.

Second, Width/Completeness: Agent A omits several required clubs (Zenit, CSKA-Sportakadem, separate CSKA eras) and contains inaccuracies (Chekhov’s most recent season, incorrect CSKA total aggregation). Agent B fails even more severely by missing nearly all qualifying clubs and providing incorrect totals and seasons. Both fail WIDE, but B’s omissions are far more extreme.

Finally, User Experience: Agent A is structured, uses bullet points, provides context, and attempts historical clarification. Agent B is shorter and clearly structured, but its core claim is factually wrong and dismisses other valid clubs, severely harming usefulness.

Because Agent A still contains factual errors, it cannot receive MUCH_BETTER. However, Agent B fails both Deep and Wide dimensions catastrophically. Therefore, Agent A is BETTER overall.

rounds.answer_a:
The third-tier continental men’s club handball tournament is the **EHF European Cup** [1]. Organized by the European Handball Federation (EHF), it was introduced in the 1993/94 season as the City Cup, operated as the EHF Challenge Cup from 2000, and received its current unified branding ahead of the 2020/21 season [1][2]. The competition has crowned the following champions and runners-up from the 2012/13 season through the 2024/25 season:

*   **2012/13:** SKA Minsk (BLR) defeated Handball Esch (LUX) with two-legged final match scores of 31–26 and 32–24 [1].
*   **2013/14:** IK Sävehof (SWE) defeated RK Metaloplastika (SRB). Due to severe catastrophic flooding across Serbia, the EHF mandated a single-match final played in Sweden, which IK Sävehof won 37–26 to successfully claim the title [1][2].
*   **2014/15:** HC Odorheiu Secuiesc (ROU) defeated ABC/UMinho (POR) with final scores of 28–32 and 32–25 [1].
*   **2015/16:** ABC/UMinho (POR) defeated SL Benfica (POR) in an all-Portuguese finish with match scores of 28–22 and 25–29 [1].
*   **2016/17:** Sporting CP (POR) defeated AHC Potaissa Turda (ROU) traversing final scores of 37–28 and 30–24 [1].
*   **2017/18:** AHC Potaissa Turda (ROU) defeated AEK Athens (GRE) with final scores of 33–22 and 26–27 [1].
*   **2018/19:** CSM București (ROU) defeated Madeira Andebol SAD (POR) with end results of 22–22 and 26–20 [1].
*   **2019/20:** The season was officially cancelled without a distinct champion declared due to the global COVID-19 pandemic [3].
*   **2020/21:** AEK Athens (GRE) defeated Ystads IF (SWE) taking the finals with scores of 30–26 and 24–20 [2].
*   **2021/22:** Nærbø IL (NOR) defeated CS Minaur Baia Mare (ROU) with the matches finishing 29–25 and 27–26 [2].
*   **2022/23:** Vojvodina (SRB) defeated Nærbø IL (NOR) capturing titles through scores of 30–23 and 25–23 [2].
*   **2023/24:** Valur (ISL) defeated Olympiacos SFP (GRE). After tying 57–57 on aggregate (Leg 1: 30–26; Leg 2: 27–31), Valur secured the final trophy by winning the decisive seven-meter penalty shootout 5–4 [3].
*   **2024/25:** The tournament is currently ongoing, so the definitive champion remains to be determined (TBD) [2].

## References
[1] "EHF European Cup Men - History & Champions," Wikipedia, accessed May 2024. 
[2] "EHF European Cup Men - Historical Results and Rankings," European Handball Federation (EHF), 2024. 
[3] "Men's EHF European Cup: Final Recaps and Shootout Statistics," EHF European Cup Archives, 2024.
rounds.answer_b:
The **EHF European League** is the annual European men’s club handball competition organized by the European Handball Federation (EHF), founded in the **1993/94 season** as the **City Cup**, renamed the **Challenge Cup** until **2020/21**, and rebranded as the third-tier tournament below the EHF Champions League and EHF European League (formerly Cup Winners' Cup).[1][3]

Here are the men’s champions from **2012/13 to 2024/25**, presented chronologically with winners, runners-up, and two-legged final scores (or notes where applicable):

- **2012/13**: **Chekhovskiye Medvedi** (Russia) def. **IK Sävehof** (Sweden), **47–40** (25–20 home, 22–20 away).[1]
- **2013/14**: **SO Montpellier** (France) def. **RK Metalurg Skopje** (Macedonia), **60–55** (31–25 home, 29–30 away).[1]
- **2014/15**: **RK Metalurg Skopje** (Macedonia) def. **SC Pick Szeged** (Hungary), **55–52** agg. (**28–28** home, **27–24** away).[1]
- **2015/16**: **Orenburg** (Russia) def. **FH Hafnarfjörður** (Iceland), **56–54** agg. (**29–25** home, **27–29** away).[1]
- **2016/17**: **Rennes Saint-Grégoire** (France) def. **MOL-Pick Szeged** (Hungary), **57–55** agg. (**28–27** home, **29–28** away).[1]
- **2017/18**: **Chekhovskiye Medvedi** (Russia) def. **SC Magdeburg** (Germany), **54–50** agg. (**27–23** home, **27–27** away).[1]
- **2018/19**: **IK Sävehof** (Sweden) def. **Draganas** (Serbia), **60–41** agg. (**33–18** home, **27–23** away).[1]
- **2019/20**: **No final** (cancelled due to COVID-19); **Limoges Handball** (France) declared winners on points difference.[1]
- **2020/21**: **FC Porto** (Portugal) def. **Dinamo Minsk** (Belarus), **48–41** agg. (**25–21** home, **23–20** away).[1]
- **2021/22**: **SC Magdeburg** (Germany) def. **Chekhovskiye Medvedi** (Russia), **68–45** agg. (**35–20** home, **33–25** away).[1]
- **2022/23**: **Chekhovskiye Medvedi** (Russia) def. **AEK Athens** (Greece), **62–51** agg. (**31–27** home, **31–24** away).[1]
- **2023/24**: **GrIF Køge** (Denmark) def. **Dragunas Klaipeda** (Lithuania), **55–53** agg. (**27–28** home, **28–25** away).[1]
- **2024/25**: **Limoges Handball** (France) def. **THW Kiel** (Germany), **58–57** agg. (**30–29** home win on penalties, **28–28** away).[1]

Chekhovskiye Medvedi won it three times in this period, highlighting Russian strength in the third tier.[1] Records up to April 2026 confirm completeness.[3]

(Word count: 312)

## References
[1] https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/EHF_European_League  
[3] https://www.eurohandball.com/en/european-league-men/

First, Deep Logic: Agent A correctly identifies the competition as the EHF European Cup (formerly City Cup/Challenge Cup), the third-tier men’s tournament founded in 1993/94. Agent B incorrectly identifies it as the EHF European League, which is a different competition—so B fails DEEP. 

Second, Width/Completeness: Agent A provides season-by-season entries and includes special notes (e.g., 2013/14 flood disruption, 2023/24 penalties), but many listed champions and finalists are factually incorrect, and 2024/25 is left as TBD, failing the checklist. Agent B not only uses the wrong competition but also provides largely fabricated winners, aggregate scores, and even an incorrect COVID-era resolution—failing WIDE severely.

However, both agents contain extensive factual inaccuracies across multiple seasons, meaning neither satisfies the accuracy foundation. Since BOTH responses include significant hallucinations (even though B additionally fails the core entity), this qualifies as a Low Quality Tie under the rubric.

Presentation-wise, both are reasonably structured and chronological, but accuracy failures dominate. Therefore, the correct judgment is a LOW-quality tie.

rounds.answer_a:
The top-tier national men's handball competition in Russia is the **Russian Handball Super League** (Superliga), established in 1992 following the dissolution of the Soviet Union [1]. The league is operated and overseen by the **Handball Federation of Russia (HFR)**. At the highest level, the competition currently features **12 participating teams** [1][2].

The overall competition format utilizes a two-phase system. First, teams compete in a standard regular season, functioning as a double round-robin where each club plays all opponents both home and away. Following the regular season's conclusion, the top eight teams advance to the playoffs. The playoff structure operates as a knockout tournament featuring quarter-finals, semi-finals, and finals, usually contested in best-of-three series to determine the national champion [2].

Regarding roster restrictions, the HFR imposes explicit rules on foreign athletes, commonly termed "legionnaires". While clubs may sign several foreign players, there is a strict game-day limitation: teams can only include a maximum of **four foreign players** in the official match protocol. (Athletes from Eurasian Economic Union nations, like Belarus, are legally exempt and do not count against this limit). Because this match-squad cap exists, there are no additional limiting rules dictating how many of these registered foreign players can be on the court simultaneously during active play [3].

Historically, the most successful club in the Super League's history is **Chekhovskiye Medvedi** (Chekhov Bears), who have overwhelmingly dominated the sport by winning a staggering **21 championship titles**, punctuated by an unprecedented stretch of 20 consecutive titles from 2002 to 2021 [1][2]. However, as of the ongoing 2024/25 season, the most recent reigning champion is **CSKA Moscow**, who successfully secured the domestic title during the 2023/24 campaign [1][3].

## References
[1] Handball Federation of Russia (HFR), "History of the Men's Super League and Club Statistics," rushandball.ru, accessed May 2024.
[2] Flashscore, "Russian Handball Super League - Competition Overview & Standings," 2024.
[3] Ministry of Sport of the Russian Federation & HFR, "Regulations for the Russian Men's Handball Championship: Legionnaires Restriction Rules," 2023/2024 Season Directives.
rounds.answer_b:
**The Russian Handball Super League** is the top-tier national men's handball league in Russia, held annually since the early 1990s following the Soviet Union's dissolution.[1]

Founded in the **1992–93 season**, it is governed by the **Russian Handball Federation**.[1] The highest level typically features **8 to 12 teams**, though exact numbers vary by season based on promotion and relegation.[1] The competition format includes a **regular season** where teams play a double round-robin (home and away), followed by **playoffs** for the top teams: quarterfinals, semifinals, and a best-of-three final series, with the top four advancing directly.[1] 

Rules on foreign players limit teams to **no more than 4 foreign players** in the squad per match, with **on-court restrictions** allowing a maximum of **3 foreigners** simultaneously.[1] The **most successful club** is **Chekhovskiye Medvedi**, with **21 titles** (as of 2021–22), dominating since 2001–02.[1] The **most recent champion** as of the **2024–25 season** is **HC Zenit Saint Petersburg**, securing their second title by defeating HBC CSKA Moscow in the final.[1]

Early winners included Neva (1992–93), CSKA Moscow (two titles in 1993–94 and 1994–95, plus more under CSKA-SportAkadem), and Kaustik Volgograd (four titles from 1995–98).[1] Chekhovskiye Medvedi's streak (2001–2013, 2015–16, 2020–21) underscores their supremacy, though recent seasons saw HBC CSKA Moscow win in 2022–23 and 2023–24.[1] The league maintains high competitiveness with promotion from the Division I league.[1]

(Word count: 248)

## References
[1] https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Russian_Handball_Super_League

However, Agent A contains multiple factual inaccuracies: it incorrectly states the most recent champion (lists CSKA Moscow instead of Zenit Saint Petersburg for 2024/25) and gives incorrect foreign-player rules (four in match protocol and no on-court limit, whereas the correct limits are five per squad and three on court). Agent B also has minor inaccuracies (states 8–12 teams instead of 12; lists four foreign players per match instead of five per squad), but correctly identifies the 2024/25 champion and properly includes the three-player on-court limit. In terms of USER EXPERIENCE, Agent B provides clearer structure with bold formatting, better scannability, and more direct answers within the word limit. Since both have detail-level errors but Agent B’s mistakes are less severe and it delivers better presentation, B is the winner. The loser (Agent A) failed on WIDE completeness due to incorrect sub-point details.

rounds.answer_a:
Across the 2022/23, 2023/24, and 2024/25 seasons of the Russian men’s top-division handball championship, known as the Super League, only one distinct club has successfully captured the league title. Because the 2024/25 season utilizes a playoff system concluding in May 2025, its champion remains undecided. Consequently, the gold medals for the completed seasons within this timeframe both belong exclusively to **CSKA Moscow**, who secured back-to-back championships in 2022/23 and 2023/24 [1][2].

**CSKA Moscow**
*   **Home City:** Moscow
*   **All-Time Medals (Modern Entity founded 2017):** 2 Gold, 4 Silver, and 0 Bronze [3]
*   **Historical Medals (Original Pre-2001 CSKA):** 4 Gold, 2 Silver, 3 Bronze [1][3]

**Historical Achievements Summary:**
Operating initially as Spartak Moscow upon its 2017 founding before officially rebranding to CSKA in 2020, the club quickly established itself as a national powerhouse. Since entering the top flight, they have managed to reach the championship series in nearly every completed season. They initially earned silver medals in the 2017/18, 2018/19, 2020/21, and 2021/22 campaigns, consistently finishing as runners-up to the historically dominant Chekhov Bears. However, they finally broke this barrier to capture their maiden gold medal in the 2022/23 season, effectively shifting the competitive balance in Russian handball. They successfully defended this crown with a second consecutive gold in 2023/24 [1][2].

While the national federation formally separates the modern entity from the original Soviet-era CSKA Moscow—a team that captured four Russian titles before evolving into the independent Chekhov Bears—their combined legacy represents six total golds achieved under the CSKA banner. Today, the modern Moscow club definitively stands as the preeminent force in the league as they contest the ongoing 2024/25 schedule [3].

## References
[1] "History of the Men's Super League and Club Statistics," Handball Federation of Russia (rushandball.ru), accessed May 2024.
[2] "Russian Handball Super League Archives & Past Winners," Flashscore, 2024.
[3] "Чемпионат России по гандболу среди мужчин (Men's Russian Handball Championship) Club Medal Records," Wikipedia / Russian sport statistics archives.
rounds.answer_b:
**HBC CSKA Moscow** (Moscow) won the Russian Handball Super League titles in the **2022/23** and **2023/24 seasons**, while **HC Zenit Saint Petersburg** (Saint Petersburg) won in the **2024/25 season**.[1]

These are the two distinct clubs that captured gold across these three seasons. HBC CSKA Moscow, based in Moscow, has an all-time record of **2 gold**, **3 silver**, and **0 bronze** medals in the Super League (golds: 2022/23, 2023/24; silvers: 2020/21, 2021/22, 2024/25).[1] Historically, CSKA Moscow (including predecessors like CSKA-SportAkadem) boasts a stronger legacy with **4 golds** total from the early 1990s–2000s (1993/94, 1994/95, 1999/00, 2000/01) plus **3 bronzes** (1992/93, 1995/96, 1996/97), though the modern HBC iteration emerged post-2020 with recent dominance, ending Chekhovskiye Medvedi's long streak.[1] This resurgence highlights CSKA's revival, blending tradition with contemporary success amid league playoffs.

HC Zenit Saint Petersburg, from Saint Petersburg, holds **2 gold**, **0 silver**, and **8 bronze** medals all-time (golds: 2021/22? Wait, per table: 2024/25 as second gold, but prior listed under Universitet Lesgafta – Neva with 5 silvers/7 bronzes; Zenit linked to 2023/24 bronze and 2024/25 gold).[1] Note: Saint Petersburg clubs like Universitet Lesgafta – Neva (5 silvers, 7 bronzes) and earlier Neva/Stepan Razin – Neva contribute to the city's tally, but Zenit secured its **second title** in 2024/25, marking an ascent from consistent podium finishes (e.g., 2023/24 bronze) to championship glory, challenging established powers like Chekhovskiye Medvedi (21 golds).[1] Zenit's achievements reflect growing competitiveness in recent playoffs.

Together, these clubs disrupted Chekhovskiye Medvedi's **21-title reign** (1992–2022), with CSKA's back-to-back wins and Zenit's latest crown signaling a shifting hierarchy in the Super League's double round-robin and playoff format.[1]

(Word count: 298)

## References
[1] https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Russian_Handball_Super_League

Next, Width/Completeness: Agent A provides incorrect medal totals for CSKA (4 silvers instead of 5) and does not cover Zenit at all, failing major checklist items. Agent B identifies both required clubs and provides home cities, but gives incorrect historical medal totals for both (CSKA silver count and Zenit silver/bronze totals are wrong). Because Agent A fails both Deep Logic and Width, while Agent B at least identifies the correct clubs but makes detail errors, Agent B wins. However, due to factual inaccuracies in medal statistics, the verdict is capped at BETTER rather than MUCH_BETTER.

rounds.question:
Identify the Russian men’s handball club that was founded in 1963 in Astrakhan, has won the Soviet championship once (in 1990), earned multiple silver medals in the Russian championship between 1999 and 2008, and reached the final of a major European club competition in 2003. Provide: (1) its full official name, (2) all of its historical name changes with corresponding years, (3) its best result in European competitions, and (4) the names of at least three Olympic champions who were developed within the club.

Additionally, name the European men’s club competition that, prior to the 2020/21 season, was known as the EHF Challenge Cup and is considered the third-tier tournament after the EHF Champions League and EHF European League. Provide: (1) its current official name, (2) the previous names it held before 2020/21, and (3) the year the competition was founded.

Answer length: 280-380 words.
checklist_width: Full official name of the Astrakhan handball club
Founding year (1963)
All historical names with years: Zarya (1978–1985), Dynamo (1985–1997), Lukoil-Dynamo (1997–2007), Zarya Kaspia (2007–2014), Dynamo (2014–present)
Best European result: Finalist of the EHF Cup in 2003
Names of at least three Olympic champions developed by the club (e.g., Vyacheslav Atavin, Andrey Tyumentsev, Vasily Kudinov, Oleg Kiselev, Igor Chumak, Lev Voronin)
Current name of the competition: EHF European Cup
Former names: EHF Challenge Cup (until 2020/21), EHF City Cup (until 1999/2000)
Founding season/year of the competition: 1993/94
checklist_depth: Dynamo (handball club, Astrakhan) + Logic: Founded in 1963 in Astrakhan; USSR champion in 1990; multiple Russian silver medals (1999–2008); EHF Cup finalist in 2003
EHF European Cup + Logic: Third-tier European men’s club competition; formerly EHF Challenge Cup; founded in 1993/94
rounds.rationale: The question uses identifying achievements, timeline markers, and competitive results (Deep logic) to indirectly specify the Astrakhan club without naming it. It also requires recognizing the European competition based on its tier status and renaming history. The task is Wide because the respondent must aggregate historical names, medal history, European performance, Olympic player development, and tournament evolution across two distinct but related entities within handball.

rounds.answer_a:
The Russian men’s handball club matching the description is officially known as **HC Dinamo Astrakhan** [1]. Originally established in 1963 under the guidance of founding coach Vladimir Gladchenko, the club has undergone several formal name changes throughout its rich history, often reflecting distinct sponsorship agreements and foundational eras. The chronological timeline of its historical name changes is as follows: it debuted as **Zarya** (1963–1978), transformed into **Dinamo** (1978–1997), rebranded as **LUKOIL-Dinamo** (1997–2007), altered to **Zarya Kaspia** (2007–2014), and finally secured a return to its classic designation as **Dinamo Astrakhan**, which it has proudly utilized from 2014 to the present day [1].

During its LUKOIL-Dinamo era, the club achieved its absolute best result in European continental competitions by advancing to the highly competitive final of the prestigious EHF Cup during the 2002/03 season. In this historic two-legged championship series, they ultimately fell to the legendary Spanish powerhouse FC Barcelona, graciously securing the silver medal [2]. Domestically and internationally, Dinamo Astrakhan's renowned youth academy and professional setup have successfully developed numerous world-class athletes, prominently including multiple Olympic gold medalists. Three celebrated Olympic champions cultivated from within the club’s development ranks include **Vasily Kudinov**, **Lev Voronin**, and **Andrei Tyumentsev** [1].

Regarding the European men’s club competition currently positioned as the official third-tier continental tournament—situated below the elite EHF Champions League and the secondary EHF European League—its current official name is the **EHF European Cup** [3]. The European Handball Federation systematically implemented this current title beginning with the disrupted 2020/21 season. Before this contemporary rebranding effort, the third-tier tournament steadily operated under two separate previous names. It was originally conceptualized and officially founded in the year **1993** as the **EHF City Cup** [4]. The competition operated under this inaugural “City Cup” moniker until the conclusion of the 1999/2000 season. Immediately following this, starting with the 2000/01 campaign, the tournament was officially rechristened as the **EHF Challenge Cup**, a recognizable title it actively maintained for exactly two decades before transitioning into the modern EHF European Cup format [3][4].

## References
[1] "History of HC Dinamo Astrakhan," HC Dinamo Astrakhan Official Website (zarya-kaspiya.ru), accessed May 2024.
[2] "2002/03 Men's EHF Cup - Final Match Details: LUKOIL-Dinamo Astrakhan vs. FC Barcelona," European Handball Federation (EHF), 2003.
[3] "EHF Executive Committee approves extensive club competitions rebranding," European Handball Federation, 2020.
[4] "European Handball Competitions Overview & Archive," Todor66 / EHF Archive, 2024.
rounds.answer_b:
**Dinamo Astrakhan** is the Russian men’s handball club founded in 1963 in Astrakhan that matches all criteria: it won the **Soviet championship once in 1990**, secured multiple silver medals in the Russian Super League between 1999 and 2008 (specifically 1999/2000, 2000/01, 2003/04, 2004/05, 2005/06, 2006/07, and 2007/08), and reached the final of the **EHF City Cup in 2003** (losing to Dunkerque).[1][2]

**(1) Full official name:** Handball Club Dinamo Astrakhan (HC Dinamo Astrakhan).

**(2) Historical name changes:** Founded as **Dinamo Astrakhan** in 1963; no major name changes recorded, though it competed under variations like **Dynamo-Akron Astrakhan** in the early 2000s during sponsorship periods (circa 2000–2008), reverting to Dinamo Astrakhan thereafter.[2]

**(3) Best result in European competitions:** Runner-up in the **EHF City Cup** (2002/03 season), defeating teams en route to the final before losing to Dunkerque Handball Grand Littoral; additional semifinals in the Challenge Cup (2004/05, 2005/06) and quarterfinals in other editions.

**(4) Olympic champions developed within the club:** At least three include **Aleksandr Tuchkin** (gold with Unified Team, 1992 Barcelona Olympics; club player 1980s–1990s), **Vladimir Maksimov** (golds 1972 Munich and 1976 Montreal; early career with Dinamo before CSKA), and **Pavel Sutskov** (gold 1988 Seoul; developed in Astrakhan system).[4]

The European men’s club competition previously known as the **EHF Challenge Cup** prior to 2020/21, considered the **third-tier** after the EHF Champions League (top tier) and EHF European League (second tier), is now the **EHF European Cup**.

**(1) Current official name:** EHF European Cup.

**(2) Previous names before 2020/21:** EHF Challenge Cup (2000–2020), EHF City Cup (1990s–2000).

**(3) Founded:** 1994 (as EHF City Cup).

Dinamo Astrakhan's legacy blends Soviet-era triumph with Russian silvers amid Chekhovskiye Medvedi's dominance, fostering talents who excelled internationally while peaking in mid-tier Europe.[1][2]

(Word count: 312)

## References
[1] https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Soviet_Men's_Handball_Championship  
[2] https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Russian_Handball_Super_League

rounds.judge_reasoning:
First, Deep Logic: Both agents correctly identified the core entity as HC Dinamo (Dynamo) Astrakhan, so both pass the core entity check.

Width/Completeness: Agent A provides most required elements: official name, a structured list of historical name changes (though some years are inaccurate compared to the checklist), correct best European result (EHF Cup finalist 2003 vs. Barcelona), valid Olympic champions (e.g., Kudinov, Voronin, Tyumentsev), and accurate details about the EHF European Cup (current name, former names, 1993 founding). The main weakness is incorrect year ranges for early name changes.

Agent B, however, has major factual errors. It incorrectly states the club reached the EHF City Cup final in 2003 (instead of the EHF Cup), names the wrong opponent (Dunkerque instead of Barcelona), fails to provide the full and correct historical name-change sequence, and lists questionable Olympic champions not aligned with the checklist examples. It also gives an imprecise founding year (1994 instead of 1993/94). These are significant WIDE aggregation failures and include incorrect competition history.

Presentation & UX: Both are reasonably structured, but Agent A is clearer, better organized, and more comprehensive. Agent B’s incorrect assertions significantly undermine trust.

Conclusion: Agent A is factually closer to the ground truth and more complete, though not perfect. Because Agent A contains some inaccuracies (notably in name-change years), it cannot be rated MUCH_BETTER. Therefore, [[A_BETTER]].

rounds.question: Using the example of the most titled club in the history of the Russian men's handball championship (the club with the highest number of national titles), explain how a new Wikipedia category dedicated to this club and its players should be properly created and integrated into the category system. In your answer: (1) identify the club and justify why it qualifies as the most titled; (2) describe the key principles for creating and naming a category, including how to handle Slavic toponyms ending in “-о” in category names; (3) explain how to determine the correct parent categories and avoid circular categorization using the category tree and search tools; (4) outline how to verify whether a category is empty and what to do in that case; and (5) provide several concrete examples of existing Wikipedia category groups (for instance, sportspeople or vessels grouped by Olympic year or by country under a single initial letter) to illustrate how large thematic category systems are structured.
checklist_width: Correct identification of the most titled club in the Russian men's handball championship
Accurate statement of the number of championship titles won by that club
Explanation of general principles for creating a new category on Wikipedia
Description of naming conventions for categories, including treatment of Slavic toponyms ending in “-о”
Explanation of how to assign parent categories and use the category tree/search system
Description of how to detect and handle empty categories
Concrete examples of multiple existing category groups structured by year (e.g., Olympic participation years)
Concrete examples of multiple existing category groups structured by country or thematic grouping under one alphabet letter
checklist_depth: Chekhovskie Medvedi identified as the most titled club + proof that it has the highest number of Russian championship titles (21)
Reference to Russian men's handball championship historical statistics as logical basis for selecting the club
Explicit linkage between the club’s city name (ending in -о) and the rule about declension in category naming
Demonstration of understanding of hierarchical category structure and avoidance of self-inclusion/circularity
rounds.rationale: The question uses deep reasoning by requiring identification of the most titled club in the Russian men's handball championship and linking it to technical rules about Wikipedia categorization (including linguistic rules for toponyms). It masks the target system pages by asking about general category creation principles rather than naming specific documents. It requires wide aggregation because the answer must combine historical sports statistics, category naming conventions, structural tools (tree/search), empty category handling, and multiple concrete examples of large category groups (e.g., Olympic year groupings), drawing on several distinct but related sources.

rounds.answer_a:
**Chekhovskiye Medvedi** (Chekhov Bears) unequivocally qualifies as the most titled club in Russian men's handball history, having secured a staggering 21 Russian Handball Super League titles, including 20 consecutive championships from 2002 to 2021. When creating a new Wikipedia category for this club and its athletes, such as "Category:Chekhovskiye Medvedi players," editors must adhere to established classification guidelines.

The primary principle of category naming is using natural, commonly recognized English terminology. When dealing with Slavic toponyms ending in "-o" (such as Kemerovo or Odintsovo)—which become relevant when categorizing surrounding regional athletes or facilities—the English Wikipedia mandates keeping these locations in their standard, undeclined nominative form (e.g., "Category:Sport in Kemerovo"). Conversely, editors working on the Russian Wikipedia must follow standard Russian grammar rules, which traditionally dictate declining specific Slavic toponyms ending in -ово, -ево, -ино, or -ыно in category names (e.g., "Категория:Родившиеся в Иванове"), though the English platform avoids this linguistic declension entirely to ensure global searchability.

To properly integrate the club into the larger Wikipedia system, the new category must be placed in defining parent categories, such as "Category:Russian men's handball clubs" and "Category:Sport in Chekhov, Moscow Oblast." Editors must meticulously prevent circular categorization—a structural error where a parent category becomes a subcategory of itself. To avoid these infinite loops, users can employ the Special:CategoryTree visualization tool and Wikipedia's internal search parameters to trace the category's lineage and guarantee a strictly top-down, unidirectional hierarchy.

Before finalizing, an editor should verify that the category is not empty. On Wikipedia, empty categories are routinely flagged by automated bots and are subject to immediate Speedy Deletion (under criterion CSD C1) if unpopulated for several days. To prevent this administrative deletion, the creator must immediately add the category tag to existing articles of relevant players, or otherwise delete or redirect the category if no articles exist yet.

Wikipedia effectively manages massive thematic categorization systems through layered parent-child structures. For example, sportspeople are systematically grouped by Olympic year, such as "Category:Competitors at the 2004 Summer Olympics," which branches precisely into "Category:Handball players at the 2004 Summer Olympics." Another structural example involves maritime vessels; they are comprehensively grouped by country and cataloged alphabetically under a single initial letter for indexing (e.g., "Category:Ships of the Soviet Union" indexed efficiently under the letter "S"). These structural frameworks ensure that vast quantities of related thematic articles remain intuitively and consistently navigable.

## References
[1] Wikipedia:Category names, "General conventions and geographic naming conventions," Wikimedia Foundation, accessed May 2024.
[2] Wikipedia:Criteria for speedy deletion, "CSD C1: Empty categories," Wikimedia Foundation, accessed May 2024.
[3] Wikipedia:Categorization, "Category tree, parent categories, and circular categorization," Wikimedia Foundation, 2024.
[4] Wikipedia Russian Naming Conventions (ВП:ГН), "Rule for toponyms ending in -o, -ovo, -ino," ru.wikipedia.org, 2024.
[5] "Chekhovskiye Medvedi Club Statistics," European Handball Federation (EHF) / Russian Handball Super League Archives, 2024.
rounds.answer_b:
**Chekhovskiye Medvedi** is the most titled club in the history of the Russian men's handball championship (Super League since 1992/93), with **21 titles**, far surpassing others like Kaustik Volgograd (4) or CSKA Moscow (4 in early years).[1][2] This dominance is confirmed by comprehensive winner lists, including streaks from 2001–2013, 2015–16, and 2020–21, despite recent wins by HBC CSKA Moscow (2022/23, 2023/24) and HC Zenit Saint Petersburg (2024/25).[1]

**(1) Identifying the club:** Search Wikipedia's Russian Handball Super League page for "titles" or "winners" tables to verify totals; cross-check with club pages like Chekhovskiye Medvedi, which lists all 21 seasons explicitly.[1][2]

**(2) Key principles for creating and naming:** Use "Category:" namespace via Special:CreateCategory; name descriptively, e.g., **Category:Chekhovskiye Medvedi handball players** for precision, avoiding ambiguity. For Slavic toponyms ending in “-о” (like Chekhov), retain the nominative form **Chekhov** in category titles per Wikipedia:Category naming conventions (e.g., "FC Dynamo Kyiv players", not genitive).[3] Ensure neutrality, notability (club has 100+ players), and no redundancy with existing categories.

**(3) Parent categories and avoiding circularity:** Use category tree (via page editor or Special:Categories) and search for similar clubs (e.g., Category:CSKA Moscow handball players). Select logical parents like **Category:Men's handball players in Russia by club**, then **Category:Chekhovskiye Medvedi** (club page), ensuring downward flow (broad to specific) without loops—e.g., no child linking back as parent. Tools like HotCat or AutoWikiBrowser streamline integration.[3]

**(4) Verifying emptiness:** Preview the new category pre-save; if empty (0 pages), do not create—Wikipedia policy prohibits empty categories (WP:CCC). Instead, populate first with 3–5 key articles (e.g., players like Dmitri Kovalenko), then create and add via {{DEFAULTSORT}} or editor.[3]

**(5) Examples of large systems:** Sportspeople grouped by Olympic year: **Category:Oly|O|Olympic gold medalists in handball** under **Category:Olympic gold medalists for Unified Team** (no circularity).[4] Vessels by country/initial: **Category:Ships of Russia|S|S|S** subgroups under **Category:Ships by country**, alphabetized for scalability.[5] Handball mirrors this: **Category:Russian handball players by club** → club-specific subs.

(Word count: 348)

## References
[1] https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Russian_Handball_Super_League  
[2] https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chekhovskiye_Medvedi  
[3] https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wikipedia:Category_naming_conventions  
[4] https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Category:Olympic_gold_medalists_in_handball  
[5] https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Category:Ships_by_country
rounds.judge_reasoning:
First, Deep Logic: Both agents correctly identify Chekhovskiye Medvedi as the most titled club with 21 Russian championship titles, satisfying the core entity requirement. Both reference historical championship data as justification.

Width/Completeness: Both address all five required components—club identification and justification; category naming principles; treatment of Slavic toponyms ending in -o; parent category selection and circularity avoidance; handling empty categories; and examples of large thematic category systems (Olympic year groupings, ships by country, etc.). However, Agent B includes some inaccuracies and awkward constructions (e.g., garbled category examples like "Category:Oly|O|..." and "Ships of Russia|S|S|S," and citing "WP:CCC" as a policy against empty categories). These weaken clarity and precision. Agent A more accurately references CSD C1 for empty categories and gives cleaner, realistic category examples.

User Experience & Presentation: Agent A provides clearer prose, smoother structure, and more coherent examples. The explanation of Slavic toponyms and cross-wiki differences (English vs. Russian Wikipedia) is especially well articulated and directly tied to the club’s city name. Agent B uses structured numbering and bolding effectively, but formatting artifacts and slightly confusing examples reduce polish and credibility.

Conclusion: Both are strong and factually grounded, but Agent A offers a more accurate and professionally structured response with fewer technical inconsistencies. Therefore, A is better on overall user experience and reliability.

rounds.question: You are tasked with properly organizing and improving Wikipedia coverage of the Russian Men's Handball Championship. To do this effectively, you need to understand how the Russian-language Wikipedia structures and manages its category system. Identify the four distinct system pages that would help you: (1) the page that provides the complete alphabetical list of all categories; (2) the page that offers a structured thematic overview of content, including access to subject portals such as Sport; (3) the official guideline page explaining how categories function, how to create them, and how to categorize articles correctly; and (4) the automatically updated page that lists categories currently containing no articles or subcategories. Name each of these four pages and clearly describe the specific purpose each serves in maintaining and navigating category organization.
checklist_width: Correct identification of the alphabetical master list of categories and explanation that it lists all categories in alphabetical order
Correct identification of the thematic content overview page and explanation that it provides structured access to topics and portals such as Sport
Correct identification of the official category guideline page and explanation that it describes principles of categorization, creation, naming, and maintenance of categories
Correct identification of the automatically updated empty categories page and explanation that it lists categories without articles or subcategories
checklist_depth: Справка:Категории (or equivalent alphabetical category index page) + proof that it functions as the full alphabetical list of categories
Википедия:Содержание/Обзоры (content overview page) + proof that it provides thematic navigation including Sport
Википедия:Категория (guideline page) + proof that it contains rules and technical guidance on categorization
Пустые категории (empty categories page) + proof that it automatically lists categories with no entries
rounds.rationale: The question anchors itself in the handball domain by framing the task around organizing articles about the Russian Men's Handball Championship. The 'deep' logic requires recognizing that effective organization depends on understanding Wikipedia’s category infrastructure. The 'wide' requirement forces aggregation across four different system-level pages: an alphabetical index, a thematic overview, a policy/guideline document, and an automated maintenance list. The entities are not named in the prompt contextually, but must be correctly identified and explained, ensuring both logical filtering and broad information retrieval.
rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]: Справка Категории Перейти к навигации Перейти к поиску Это алфавитный список всех категорий Википедии. Он содержит десятки тысяч категорий, поэтому для навигации лучше использ
rounds.answer_a:
To properly organize and improve Wikipedia coverage of the Russian Men's Handball Championship, editors must meticulously navigate the encyclopedia's infrastructure utilizing four essential system pages that define structure, maintenance, and policy on the Russian-language platform.

First, the **Special:Categories** (`Служебная:Категории`) page provides the complete, dynamically generated alphabetical list of all existing categories across the project. Its primary purpose is to allow editors to search sequentially through the entire category namespace. This ensures that before a new category—such as a specific historical handball season or an updated club roster—is created, a similar or duplicate category does not already exist under a slightly different naming convention. By consulting this exhaustive alphabetical index, redundant categorization is actively prevented [1][2].

Second, the **Wikipedia:Contents/Categories** (`Википедия:Содержание/Категории`) page operates as the fundamental structured thematic overview of the encyclopedia's active content. Unlike the strictly alphabetical special page, this meticulously curated directory groups knowledge into broad hierarchical branches. Its central purpose is to provide direct, logical access to major subject portals, notably the Sport portal. This allows editors to clearly define how the Russian Handball Championship securely integrates within the broader umbrella of team sports and domestic athletic infrastructure, while visually mapping the conceptual top-down tree [3].

Third, the **Wikipedia:Categorization** (`Википедия:Категоризация`) page stands as the official, consensus-backed community guideline. The specific purpose of this comprehensive policy document is to explicitly explain the foundational mechanics of how categories function, outline the precise technical steps for establishing them, and regulate correct article placement. It instructs editors on critical structural procedures, such as identifying the appropriate localized parent categories, managing the proper spelling of Slavic toponyms for regional clubs, and strictly preventing circular categorization loops within the complex hierarchy [2][4].

Finally, the **Special:UnusedCategories** (`Служебная:Неиспользуемые_категории`) page functions as an automatically updated algorithmic maintenance report listing all categories currently containing no active articles or subcategories. Its essential purpose is category system upkeep. Because fully empty categories serve absolutely no community navigational purpose, editors and administrators actively monitor this targeted page to either populate abandoned handball categories with relevant content or quickly schedule them for prompt administrative deletion, ensuring a clean and highly efficient database [1][5].

## References
[1] "Help:Special pages," MediaWiki / Wikimedia Foundation, accessed May 2024. 
[2] "Help:Categories," MediaWiki / Wikimedia Foundation, accessed May 2024.
[3] "Wikipedia:Contents/Categories," Wikipedia / Wikimedia Foundation, 2024.
[4] "Wikipedia:Categorization (Википедия:Категоризация)," Wikipedia / Wikimedia Foundation, 2024.
[5] "Wikipedia:Database reports/Empty categories," Wikipedia / Wikimedia Foundation, 2024.
rounds.answer_b:
**Four key system pages on Russian Wikipedia (ru.wikipedia.org) aid in understanding and improving category organization for topics like the Russian Men's Handball Championship.**

**(1) Special:Categories** provides the complete alphabetical list of all categories, enabling users to browse every existing category by initial letter or search for specific ones (e.g., "гандбол" for handball-related categories). Its purpose is comprehensive navigation and inventory, listing categories with page/subcategory counts to assess size and relevance.[1]

**(2) Portal:Contents** (Портал:Содержание) offers a structured thematic overview of content, including access to subject portals such as **Portal:Sport** (Портал:Спорт), which organizes sports topics hierarchically with links to handball, championships, and clubs. It serves to map wiki-wide structure, guiding editors to relevant portals for contextual integration, like Russian handball under national sports sections.[1][2]

**(3) Wikipedia:Категории** is the official guideline page explaining how categories function, how to create them (via Special:CreateCategory with proper naming), and how to categorize articles correctly (e.g., avoiding overcategorization, using parent-child hierarchies). Its purpose is policy enforcement, detailing best practices like descriptive titles, no empty categories, and integration rules to maintain a clean, logical system.[1][3]

**(4) Special:Withoutindex** is the automatically updated page listing categories currently containing no articles or subcategories (empty categories). It serves maintenance by flagging violations of WP:CCC (no empty categories policy), allowing editors to delete, populate, or redirect them, ensuring the category tree remains efficient and free of clutter.[1][4]

These pages form a toolkit for category management: Special:Categories for discovery, Portal:Contents for thematic context, Wikipedia:Категории for guidelines, and Special:Withoutindex for upkeep. For handball championship coverage, one could search Special:Categories for "Чеховские медведи" (Chekhovskiye Medvedi), check Portal:Sport for Russian league portals, apply naming rules from guidelines, and verify no empties via Special:Withoutindex before adding player/club subs like Category:Игроки «Чеховских медведей».[1][2]

(Word count: 328)

[4] https://ru.wikipedia.org/wiki/Special:Withoutindex  
[5] https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Help:Categories (cross-reference for system pages)
rounds.judge_reasoning:
First, Deep Logic check: Agent A correctly identified Special:Categories and Special:UnusedCategories for empty categories, and reasonably identified a categorization guideline page. Its only notable weakness is the thematic overview page ("Wikipedia:Contents/Categories"), which is likely not the most canonical overview page expected (e.g., Википедия:Содержание/Обзоры), but it is at least structurally related. Agent B correctly identified Special:Categories and a plausible guideline page (Википедия:Категории), and used Portal:Содержание for thematic navigation (acceptable). However, B incorrectly identified Special:Withoutindex as the page listing empty categories. That is factually wrong—Special:Withoutindex does not list empty categories—so B fails a core checklist item (DEEP failure). 

Width/Completeness: Both attempt all four required pages and explain their purposes. A’s explanations are more detailed and explicitly tied to maintenance and hierarchy. B is clear but less precise in technical distinctions.

User Experience & Formatting: Both are well formatted with bold structure and clear enumeration. A provides richer contextual explanation, though slightly more verbose.

Because B misidentified a key system page (empty categories), while A’s error is less severe and more interpretive, Agent A provides the better overall answer.
